It can be verified that exp⁡(ρ∗​(X))\exp(\rho_{*}(X)) is conjugate to an integer matrix. Therefore, by Theorem 3.2, there is a non-Kähler SYZ mirror pair with base G/ΓG/\Gamma, where Γ=exp⁡(γ)\Gamma=\exp(\gamma).

4. On the Tseng-Yau cohomology

Let (X˘,ω˘)(\breve{X},\breve{\omega}) be a symplectic manifold and let 𝜶\boldsymbol{\alpha} denote the bivector field dual to the symplectic form ω˘\breve{\omega}. In [Tseng_2012], Tseng and Yau introduced two cohomology theories that are sensitive to the symplectic structure. Specifically, they are invariant under symplectomorphisms but not necessarily under diffeomorphisms, and they are finite-dimensional on compact manifolds.

Let (Ω∙​(X˘,ℂ),d)(\Omega^{\bullet}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}),\mathrm{d}) be the de Rham complex. Set Λ=ι​(𝜶)\Lambda=\iota(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) as the interior product with 𝜶\boldsymbol{\alpha}, and let dΛ:Ωk​(X˘,ℂ)→Ωk−1​(X˘,ℂ)\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\Omega^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\to\Omega^{k-1}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}) be given by dΛ=[d,Λ]\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}=[\mathrm{d},\Lambda]. It is straightforward to check that:

(120) ddΛ=−dΛ​d,(d+dΛ)∘ddΛ=0.\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}=-\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\mathrm{d},\quad(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda})\circ\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}=0.

Consider the complex:

(121) Ωk​(X˘,ℂ)→ddΛΩk​(X˘,ℂ)→d+dΛΩk+1​(X˘,ℂ)⊕Ωk−1​(X˘,ℂ).\Omega^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\xrightarrow{\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}\Omega^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\xrightarrow{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}\Omega^{k+1}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\oplus\Omega^{k-1}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}).

The Tseng-Yau cohomology is defined as:

(122) Hd+dΛk​(X˘)=ker⁡(d+dΛ:Ωk​(X˘,ℂ)⟶Ωk+1​(X˘,ℂ)⊕Ωk−1​(X˘,ℂ))Im⁡(ddΛ:Ωk​(X˘,ℂ)⟶Ωk​(X˘,ℂ)).H^{k}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X})=\frac{\ker\left(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\Omega^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\longrightarrow\Omega^{k+1}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\oplus\Omega^{k-1}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)}{\operatorname{Im}\left(\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\Omega^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\longrightarrow\Omega^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)}.

Now reverse the arrows in the complex (121):

(123)

The cohomology of the complex (123) is defined as

(124) HddΛk​(X˘)=ker⁡(ddΛ:Ωk​(X˘,ℂ)⟶Ωk​(X˘,ℂ))(Im⁡d+Im⁡dΛ)∩Ωk​(X˘,ℂ).H^{k}_{\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X})=\frac{\ker\left(\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\Omega^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\longrightarrow\Omega^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)}{\left(\operatorname{Im}\,\mathrm{d}+\operatorname{Im}\,\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\right)\cap\Omega^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})}.

It turns out that whenever X˘\breve{X} is compact, both cohomologies coincide:

Theorem 4.1 (Tseng, Yau [Tseng_2012, Corollary 3.25]).  Let (X˘,ω˘)(\breve{X},\breve{\omega}) be a compact symplectic manifold of dimension 2​n2n. Then the Hodge star operator induces the following isomorphism:

(125) Hd+dΛk​(X˘)≃HddΛ2​n−k​(X˘)H^{k}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X})\simeq H^{2n-k}_{\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X})

for all 0≤k≤2​n0\leq k\leq 2n.

Drawing inspiration from Brylinski’s definition of Poisson cohomology [Brylinski1988] and building upon the foundational work of Connes [Connes], Kontsevich [Kontsevich2008], and Nest and Tsygan [NestTsyganCyclic, Kontsevich2008], this section introduces a bicomplex construction designed to recover Tseng-Yau cohomology. Our ultimate aim is to explore its potential connection to the periodic cyclic homology of a given dg-algebra; although this correspondence remains a subject for future work. Furthermore, we prove that our constructions are compatible with the Fourier-Mukai transform and, by extension, with non-Kähler SYZ mirror symmetry in the sense of Lau-Tseng-Yau.

4.1. Brylinski’s and Tseng-Yau’s bicomplexes

Consider the following operators on the de Rham complex (Ω∙​(X˘,ℂ),d)(\Omega^{\bullet}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}),\mathrm{d}):

  • •

    B:=d:Ωk​(X˘,ℂ)→Ωk+1​(X˘,ℂ)B:=\mathrm{d}\colon\Omega^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\to\Omega^{k+1}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}), the standard de Rham differential (degree +1+1).

  • •

    b:=dΛ:Ωk​(X˘,ℂ)→Ωk−1​(X˘,ℂ)b:=\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\Omega^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\to\Omega^{k-1}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}), where dΛ=[d,Λ]\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}=[\mathrm{d},\Lambda] (degree −1-1).

Note that dΛ\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda} coincides with the Koszul differential [Brylinski1988]. These operators (B,b)(B,b) naturally define the (Ω∙​(X˘,ℂ),b,B)(\Omega^{\bullet}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}),b,B) Brylinski’s bicomplex, since:

(126) B2=d2=0,b2=(dΛ)2=0,andB​b+b​B=ddΛ+dΛ​d=0.B^{2}=\mathrm{d}^{2}=0,\quad b^{2}=(\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda})^{2}=0,\quad\text{and}\quad Bb+bB=\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\mathrm{d}=0.

Let uu be a formal variable of degree +2+2. We consider the “positive” complex C1C_{1}:

(127) C1∙=(Ω∙​(X˘,ℂ)​((u)),D1=d+u​dΛ)C^{\bullet}_{1}=\left(\Omega^{\bullet}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})((u)),D_{1}=\mathrm{d}+u\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\right)

with a total differential D1D_{1} of degree +1+1 satisfying D12=0D_{1}^{2}=0. It can be straightforwardly proven555We have chosen to omit this proof as it would deviate too much from our goals. that there exists an isomorphism of graded vector spaces:

(128) H​(C1∙)≅Hd​R​(X˘,ℂ)​((u)).H(C^{\bullet}_{1})\cong H_{dR}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})((u)).

Moreover, let A=C∞​(X˘,ℂ)A=C^{\infty}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}) be the Fréchet algebra of smooth functions on (X˘,ω˘)(\breve{X},\breve{\omega}). We can actually check that there is an isomorphism of graded vector spaces:

(129) H​(C1∙)≅H​P∙​(A).H(C^{\bullet}_{1})\cong HP_{\bullet}(A).

Here, H​P∙​(A)HP_{\bullet}(A) denotes Connes’ periodic cyclic homology for topological algebras, i.e., the underlying continuous Hochschild complex is constructed taking into account the Fréchet topology of AA, utilizing the completed projective tensor product C∞​(X˘)​⊗^π​C∞​(X˘)≅C∞​(X˘×X˘)C^{\infty}(\breve{X})\hat{\otimes}_{\pi}C^{\infty}(\breve{X})\cong C^{\infty}(\breve{X}\times\breve{X}) [Connes, NestTsyganCyclic].

We use the isomorphism (129) as inspiration to define the Tseng-Yau bicomplex C∙=(Ω∙​(X˘,ℂ)​((u)),d+u​dΛ,ddΛ)C^{\bullet}=(\Omega^{\bullet}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})((u)),\mathrm{d}+u\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda},\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}):

4.2. New cohomologies

The Tseng-Yau bicomplex have to it associated four distinct cohomologies:

  1. (1)

    ddΛ\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda} Cohomology:

    (130) Hsk​(C∙):=ker⁡(ddΛ:Ck⟶Ck)Im⁡(ddΛ:Ck⟶Ck)H_{s}^{k}(C^{\bullet}):=\frac{\ker(\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon C^{k}\longrightarrow C^{k})}{\operatorname{Im}(\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon C^{k}\longrightarrow C^{k})}
  2. (2)

    Periodic Cyclic Cohomology (P.C.):

    (131) H​Pk​(C∙):=ker⁡(d+u​dΛ:Ck⟶Ck+1)Im⁡(d+u​dΛ:Ck−1⟶Ck)HP^{k}(C^{\bullet}):=\frac{\ker(\mathrm{d}+u\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon C^{k}\longrightarrow C^{k+1})}{\operatorname{Im}(\mathrm{d}+u\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon C^{k-1}\longrightarrow C^{k})}
  3. (3)

    Bott-Chern Cohomology:

    (132) Hd+dΛk​(C∙):=ker⁡(d+u​dΛ:Ck⟶Ck+1)Im⁡(ddΛ:Ck⟶Ck)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{k}(C^{\bullet}):=\frac{\ker(\mathrm{d}+u\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon C^{k}\longrightarrow C^{k+1})}{\operatorname{Im}(\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon C^{k}\longrightarrow C^{k})}
  4. (4)

    Aeppli Cohomology:

    (133) HddΛk​(C∙):=ker⁡(ddΛ:Ck⟶Ck)Im⁡(d+u​dΛ:Ck−1⟶Ck)H_{\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{k}(C^{\bullet}):=\frac{\ker(\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon C^{k}\longrightarrow C^{k})}{\operatorname{Im}(\mathrm{d}+u\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon C^{k-1}\longrightarrow C^{k})}

The cohomologies Hd+dΛk​(C∙)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{k}(C^{\bullet}) and HddΛk​(C∙)H_{\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{k}(C^{\bullet}) parallel the classical Tseng-Yau cohomologies Hd+dΛk​(X˘)H^{k}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X}) and HddΛk​(X˘)H^{k}_{\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X}) developed in [Tseng_2012]. The primary distinction lies in the fact that Hd+dΛ​(C∙)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(C^{\bullet}) captures mixed-degree forms. Specifically, classes in Hd+dΛk​(C∙)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{k}(C^{\bullet}) take the form [A2​i−A2​(i−1)][A_{2i}-A_{2(i-1)}] where Ai∈Ωk−2​i​(X˘,ℂ)A_{i}\in\Omega^{k-2i}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}), whereas classes in Hd+dΛk​(X˘)H^{k}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X}) consist of forms of pure degree kk. More precisely, we have the following isomorphisms:

Theorem 4.2.  Let (X˘,ω˘)(\breve{X},\breve{\omega}) be a symplectic manifold. There exist subspaces Qeven⊂Hd+dΛeven​(C∙)Q_{\mathrm{even}}\subset H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{\mathrm{even}}(C^{\bullet}) and Qodd⊂Hd+dΛodd​(C∙)Q_{\mathrm{odd}}\subset H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{\mathrm{odd}}(C^{\bullet}) such that we have the following isomorphisms of graded vector spaces:

(134) Hd+dΛeven​(C∙)\displaystyle H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{\mathrm{even}}(C^{\bullet}) ≅(⨁m​evenHd+dΛm​(X˘,ℂ))⊕Qeven,\displaystyle\cong\left(\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)\oplus Q_{\mathrm{even}},
(135) Hd+dΛodd​(C∙)\displaystyle H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{\mathrm{odd}}(C^{\bullet}) ≅(⨁m​oddHd+dΛm​(X˘,ℂ))⊕Qodd.\displaystyle\cong\left(\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{odd}}H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)\oplus Q_{\mathrm{odd}}.
Proof.

We detail the proof for the even case; the odd case follows analogously.

Using the canonical identification between CkC^{k} and ⨁i∈ℤΩk−2​i​(X˘,ℂ)\bigoplus_{i\in{\mathbb{Z}}}\Omega^{k-2i}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}), we observe that:

(136) ker⁡(d+u​dΛ:Ck→Ck+1)≅ker⁡(d+dΛ:⨁i∈ℤΩk−2​i​(X˘,ℂ)⟶⨁i∈ℤΩk+1−2​i​(X˘,ℂ))\ker(\mathrm{d}+u\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon C^{k}\to C^{k+1})\cong\ker\left(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\bigoplus_{i\in\mathbb{Z}}\Omega^{k-2i}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{i\in\mathbb{Z}}\Omega^{k+1-2i}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)

and

(137) Im⁡(ddΛ:Ck→Ck)≅⨁m​evenIm⁡(ddΛ:Ωm​(X˘,ℂ)→Ωm​(X˘,ℂ)).\operatorname{Im}(\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon C^{k}\to C^{k})\cong\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}\operatorname{Im}(\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\Omega^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\to\Omega^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})).

Consequently, we define the quotient space VV as:

(138) V:=ker⁡(d+dΛ:⨁i∈ℤΩk−2​i​(X˘,ℂ)⟶⨁i∈ℤΩk+1−2​i​(X˘,ℂ))⨁m​evenIm⁡(ddΛ:Ωm​(X˘,ℂ)→Ωm​(X˘,ℂ)).V:=\frac{\ker\left(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\bigoplus_{i\in\mathbb{Z}}\Omega^{k-2i}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{i\in\mathbb{Z}}\Omega^{k+1-2i}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)}{\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}\operatorname{Im}(\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\Omega^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\to\Omega^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}))}.

Let A=∑k=0n[A2​k]∈⨁m​evenHd+dΛm​(X˘,ℂ)A=\sum_{k=0}^{n}[A_{2k}]\in\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}), with [A2​k]∈Hd+dΛ2​k​(X˘,ℂ)[A_{2k}]\in H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{2k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}) for all 0≤k≤n0\leq k\leq n. Our goal is to prove that ⨁m​evenHd+dΛm​(X˘,ℂ)⊂V\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}H^{m}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\subset V.

Note that A2​k∈ker⁡(d)∩ker⁡(dΛ)∩Ω2​k​(X˘,ℂ)A_{2k}\in\ker(\mathrm{d})\cap\ker(\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda})\cap\Omega^{2k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}). Thus, applying the operator yields:

(139) (d+dΛ)​(∑k=0nA2​k)\displaystyle(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda})\left(\sum_{k=0}^{n}A_{2k}\right) =∑k=1n(d​A2​k+dΛ​A2​(k−1))=0\displaystyle=\sum^{n}_{k=1}\left(\mathrm{d}A_{2k}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}A_{2(k-1)}\right)=0
(140) ⟹∑k=0nA2​k∈ker⁡(d+dΛ:⨁i∈ℤΩk−2​i​(X˘,ℂ)⟶⨁i∈ℤΩk+1−2​i​(X˘,ℂ)).\displaystyle\implies\sum_{k=0}^{n}A_{2k}\in\ker\left(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\bigoplus_{i\in\mathbb{Z}}\Omega^{k-2i}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{i\in\mathbb{Z}}\Omega^{k+1-2i}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right).

This implies that ∑k=0nA2​k\sum_{k=0}^{n}A_{2k} belongs to the kernel of d+dΛ\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda} on the direct sum.

Next, suppose A,A′∈⨁m​evenHd+dΛm​(X˘,ℂ)A,A^{\prime}\in\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}H^{m}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}) such that A−A′=∑k=0nddΛ​B2​kA-A^{\prime}=\sum^{n}_{k=0}\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}B_{2k}, where B2​k∈Ω2​k​(X˘,ℂ)B_{2k}\in\Omega^{2k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}) for all kk. It immediately follows that:

(141) A−A′∈⨁m​evenIm⁡(ddΛ:Ωm​(X˘,ℂ)→Ωm​(X˘,ℂ))A-A^{\prime}\in\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}\operatorname{Im}(\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\Omega^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\to\Omega^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}))

so the inclusion mapping i:⨁m​evenHd+dΛm​(X˘,ℂ)↪Vi\colon\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}H^{m}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\hookrightarrow V is well-defined. We now show that ii is injective.

Assume i​(A)=[∑k=0nA2​k]=0i(A)=\left[\sum^{n}_{k=0}A_{2k}\right]=0. This forces:

(142) ∑k=0nA2​k\displaystyle\sum^{n}_{k=0}A_{2k} ∈⨁m​evenIm⁡(ddΛ:Ωm​(X˘,ℂ)→Ωm​(X˘,ℂ))\displaystyle\in\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}\operatorname{Im}(\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\Omega^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\to\Omega^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}))
(143) ⟹A2​k∈Im⁡(ddΛ:Ω2​k​(X˘,ℂ)→Ω2​k​(X˘,ℂ))\displaystyle\implies A_{2k}\in\operatorname{Im}(\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\Omega^{2k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\to\Omega^{2k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}))

for each kk. Thus, A=0A=0, which implies that ⨁m​evenHd+dΛm​(X˘,ℂ)⊂V\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}H^{m}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\subset V.

By completing the basis of VV, we can find a complementary subspace Qeven⊂VQ_{\mathrm{even}}\subset V such that:

(144) V=(⨁m​evenHd+dΛm​(X˘,ℂ))⊕Qeven.V=\left(\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}H^{m}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)\oplus Q_{\mathrm{even}}.

Equation (138) then yields the desired isomorphism:

(145) Hd+dΛeven​(C∙)≅(⨁m​evenHd+dΛm​(X˘,ℂ))⊕Qeven.H^{\mathrm{even}}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(C^{\bullet})\cong\left(\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}H^{m}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)\oplus Q_{\mathrm{even}}.

∎

A drawback of the cohomology Hd+dΛ​(C∙)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(C^{\bullet}) is that the QQ-term is large, making the space infinite-dimensional even on compact manifolds, as the following example shows.

Example 4.3.  Let (X˘,ω˘)(\breve{X},\breve{\omega}) be a Kähler manifold of real dimension 22. Then

(146) Hd+dΛodd​(C∙)\displaystyle H^{\mathrm{odd}}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(C^{\bullet}) =Hd+dΛ1​(X˘,ℂ)\displaystyle=H^{1}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})
(147) Hd+dΛeven​(C∙)\displaystyle H^{\mathrm{even}}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(C^{\bullet}) ≃{f+A2|d​(f+Λ​A2)=0,f∈C∞​(X˘,ℂ),A2∈Ω2​(X˘,ℂ)}\displaystyle\simeq\left\{f+A_{2}\;\middle|\;\mathrm{d}(f+\Lambda A_{2})=0,\,f\in C^{\infty}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}),\,A_{2}\in\Omega^{2}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right\}

where Λ=ι​(ω˘−1)\Lambda=\iota(\breve{\omega}^{-1}).

However, it is notable that if we restrict this cohomology to primitive forms, we recover the primitive version of the Tseng-Yau cohomology; this is the content of Theorem 4.2. Let us proceed by recalling some needed concepts.

Recall the definition of the dual Lefschetz operator:

(148) Λ:Ωk​(X˘,ℂ)\displaystyle\Lambda\colon\Omega^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}) ⟶Ωk−2​(X˘,ℂ)\displaystyle\longrightarrow\Omega^{k-2}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})
(149) A\displaystyle A ⟼Λ​A:=ι​(𝜶)​A.\displaystyle\longmapsto\Lambda A:=\iota(\boldsymbol{\alpha})A.

Definition 4.4.  A primitive form B∈Ωm​(X˘,ℂ)B\in\Omega^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}) is an mm-form such that Λ​(B)=0\Lambda(B)=0. In what follows, we denote by 𝒫m​(X˘,ℂ)\mathcal{P}^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}) the space of primitive mm-forms, and by 𝒫​(X˘,ℂ)=⨁i=02​n𝒫i​(X˘,ℂ)\mathcal{P}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})=\bigoplus_{i=0}^{2n}\mathcal{P}^{i}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}) the space of all primitive forms.

Tseng and Yau defined in [Tseng_2012] a primitive version of Hd+dΛ​(X˘)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X}):

(150) P​Hd+dΛk​(X˘):=ker⁡(d+dΛ:Ωk​(X˘,ℂ)⟶Ωk+1​(X˘,ℂ)⊕Ωk−1​(X˘,ℂ))∩𝒫k​(X˘,ℂ)Im⁡(ddΛ:Ωk​(X˘,ℂ)⟶Ωk​(X˘,ℂ))∩𝒫k​(X˘,ℂ).PH_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{k}(\breve{X}):=\frac{\ker\left(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\Omega^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\longrightarrow\Omega^{k+1}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\oplus\Omega^{k-1}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)\cap\mathcal{P}^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})}{\operatorname{Im}\left(\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\Omega^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\longrightarrow\Omega^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)\cap\mathcal{P}^{k}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})}.

Similarly, we can define the notion of primitive cohomology for H​P​(C∙)HP(C^{\bullet}):

Definition 4.5.  For the complex C∙=(Ω​(X˘,ℂ)​((u)),d+u​dΛ,ddΛ)C^{\bullet}=(\Omega(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})((u)),\mathrm{d}+u\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda},\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}), using the identification Ck≃⨁i∈ℤΩk−2​i​(X˘,ℂ)C^{k}\simeq\bigoplus_{i\in{\mathbb{Z}}}\Omega^{k-2i}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}), one can define the primitive Bott-Chern cohomology as:

(151) P​Hd+dΛk​(C∙):=ker⁡(d+dΛ:⨁m∈ℤΩk−2​m​(X˘,ℂ)⟶⨁m∈ℤΩk−2​m​(X˘,ℂ))∩𝒫​(X˘,ℂ)(⨁m∈ℤIm⁡(ddΛ:Ωk−2​m​(X˘,ℂ)⟶Ωk−2​m​(X˘,ℂ)))∩𝒫​(X˘,ℂ).PH_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{k}(C^{\bullet}):=\frac{\ker\left(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\bigoplus_{m\in{\mathbb{Z}}}\Omega^{k-2m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{m\in{\mathbb{Z}}}\Omega^{k-2m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)\cap\mathcal{P}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})}{\left(\bigoplus_{m\in{\mathbb{Z}}}\operatorname{Im}\left(\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\Omega^{k-2m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\longrightarrow\Omega^{k-2m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)\right)\cap\mathcal{P}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})}.

Proposition 4.6.  For the primitive Bott-Chern cohomology (Definition 4.2), we have for each k≥0k\geq 0:

(152) P​Hd+dΛk​(C∙)=ker⁡(d+dΛ:⨁m∈ℤΩk−2​m​(X˘,ℂ)⟶⨁m∈ℤΩk−2​m​(X˘,ℂ))∩𝒫​(X˘,ℂ)⨁m∈ℤddΛ​(𝒫k−2​m​(X˘,ℂ)).PH_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{k}(C^{\bullet})=\frac{\ker\left(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\bigoplus_{m\in{\mathbb{Z}}}\Omega^{k-2m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{m\in{\mathbb{Z}}}\Omega^{k-2m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)\cap\mathcal{P}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})}{\bigoplus_{m\in{\mathbb{Z}}}\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\left(\mathcal{P}^{k-2m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)}.
Proof.

This is a direct consequence of [Tseng_2012, Lemma 3.9]. ∎

Theorem 4.7.  Let (X˘,ω˘)(\breve{X},\breve{\omega}) be a symplectic manifold of dimension 2​n2n. Then:

(153) P​Hd+dΛk​(C∙)≅{⨁m​evenP​Hd+dΛm​(X˘,ℂ),if ​k​ is even,⨁m​oddP​Hd+dΛm​(X˘,ℂ),if ​k​ is odd.PH^{k}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(C^{\bullet})\cong\begin{cases}\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}PH_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}),&\text{if }k\text{ is even,}\\[8.61108pt] \bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{odd}}PH_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}),&\text{if }k\text{ is odd.}\end{cases}
Proof.

We present the proof for the even case, as the odd case is analogous.

Let α=∑i=0nB2​i∈⨁m​even𝒫m​(X˘,ℂ)\alpha=\sum^{n}_{i=0}B_{2i}\in\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}\mathcal{P}^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}), where B2​i∈𝒫2​i​(X˘,ℂ)B_{2i}\in\mathcal{P}^{2i}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}) for 0≤i≤n0\leq i\leq n. Observe that applying the operator (d+dΛ)(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}) yields:

(154) (d+dΛ)​(α)=∑i=1n−1(d​B2​i+dΛ​B2​(i+1)).(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda})(\alpha)=\sum^{n-1}_{i=1}\left(\mathrm{d}B_{2i}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}B_{2(i+1)}\right).

For primitive forms, we have the identity dΛ​B2​k=Λ​d​B2​k\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}B_{2k}=\Lambda\mathrm{d}B_{2k}. Substituting this into the sum gives:

(155) (d+dΛ)​(α)=∑i=1n−1(d​B2​i+Λ​d​B2​(i+1)).(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda})(\alpha)=\sum^{n-1}_{i=1}\left(\mathrm{d}B_{2i}+\Lambda\mathrm{d}B_{2(i+1)}\right).

Therefore, (d+dΛ)​(α)=0(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda})(\alpha)=0 if and only if:

(156) d​B2​i+Λ​d​B2​(i+1)=0for all ​1≤i≤n−1.\mathrm{d}B_{2i}+\Lambda\mathrm{d}B_{2(i+1)}=0\quad\text{for all }1\leq i\leq n-1.

We can show that this system of equations holds if and only if d​B2​i=0\mathrm{d}B_{2i}=0 for all 0≤i≤n0\leq i\leq n. We proceed by descending induction on the index ii. Because X˘\breve{X} has dimension 2​n2n, any form of degree strictly greater than 2​n2n must vanish; thus, B2​(n+1)=0B_{2(n+1)}=0. Consequently, for i=ni=n, the equation reduces to d​B2​n=0\mathrm{d}B_{2n}=0. Assuming d​B2​(i+1)=0\mathrm{d}B_{2(i+1)}=0 for some ii, the relation d​B2​i+Λ​d​B2​(i+1)=0\mathrm{d}B_{2i}+\Lambda\mathrm{d}B_{2(i+1)}=0 implies d​B2​i=0\mathrm{d}B_{2i}=0. By induction, d​B2​i=0\mathrm{d}B_{2i}=0 for all 0≤i≤n0\leq i\leq n.

It follows that (d+dΛ)​(α)=0(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda})(\alpha)=0 if and only if d​B2​i=0\mathrm{d}B_{2i}=0 for all 0≤i≤n0\leq i\leq n. This implies that the kernel of (d+dΛ)(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}) on even-degree primitive forms decomposes completely:

(157) ker\displaystyle\ker (d+dΛ:⨁m​evenΩm(X˘,ℂ)⟶⨁m​oddΩm(X˘,ℂ))∩𝒫(X˘,ℂ)\displaystyle\left(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\colon\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}\Omega^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\longrightarrow\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{odd}}\Omega^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)\cap\mathcal{P}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})
(158) =⨁m​evenker⁡(d:Ωm​(X˘,ℂ)→Ωm+1​(X˘,ℂ))∩𝒫​(X˘,ℂ).\displaystyle=\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}\ker\left(\mathrm{d}\colon\Omega^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\to\Omega^{m+1}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)\cap\mathcal{P}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}).

Taking the quotient by the image of ddΛ\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}, we obtain the desired isomorphism:

(159) P​Hd+dΛeven​(C∙)≅⨁m​evenker⁡(d:Ωm​(X˘,ℂ)→Ωm+1​(X˘,ℂ))∩𝒫​(X˘,ℂ)⨁m​evenddΛ​(𝒫m​(X˘,ℂ))=⨁m​evenP​Hd+dΛm​(X˘,ℂ).PH^{\mathrm{even}}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(C^{\bullet})\cong\frac{\displaystyle\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}\ker\left(\mathrm{d}\colon\Omega^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\to\Omega^{m+1}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)\cap\mathcal{P}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})}{\displaystyle\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\left(\mathcal{P}^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\right)}=\bigoplus_{m~\mathrm{even}}PH_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{m}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}).

∎

4.3. Complex cohomologies

Here we build on the discussion established in the previous section analogously aiming at obtaining a “mirror cohomology” to Hd+dΛ​(C∙)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(C^{\bullet}) for a given non-Kähler SYZ mirror pair.

Let XX be a complex manifold of dimension 2​n2n, and consider the complex:

(160) G∙:…→∂Ω0,n(X)→∂Ω0,n−1(X)⊕Ω1,n(X)→∂Ω0,n−2(X)⊕Ω1,n−1(X)⊕Ω2,n(X)→∂…G^{\bullet}\colon\quad\dots\xrightarrow{\partial}\Omega^{0,n}(X)\xrightarrow{\partial}\Omega^{0,n-1}(X)\oplus\Omega^{1,n}(X)\xrightarrow{\partial}\Omega^{0,n-2}(X)\oplus\Omega^{1,n-1}(X)\oplus\Omega^{2,n}(X)\xrightarrow{\partial}\dots

Defining Gk=⨁p=0kΩp,n−k+p​(X)G^{k}=\bigoplus_{p=0}^{k}\Omega^{p,n-k+p}(X), we equip G∙G^{\bullet} with the bicomplex structure G∙=(G,∂,∂¯)G^{\bullet}=(G,\partial,\overline{\partial}). Observe that ∂(Gk)⊂Gk+1\partial(G^{k})\subset G^{k+1} and ∂¯​(Gk)⊂Gk−1\overline{\partial}(G^{k})\subset G^{k-1}. Hence, the operator ∂\partial carries a degree of +1+1, while ∂¯\overline{\partial} carries a degree of −1-1.

Next, let uu be a formal variable of degree +2+2. We define the formal complex C^∙:=(G∙​((u)),∂+u​∂¯)\hat{C}^{\bullet}:=(G^{\bullet}((u)),\partial+u\overline{\partial}). The total differential ∂+u​∂¯\partial+u\overline{\partial} has degree +1+1, and the kk-th term of the complex is given by:

(161) C^k={∑i∈ℤui​gi|gi∈Gk−2​i​ for all ​i∈ℤ}.\hat{C}^{k}=\left\{\sum_{i\in\mathbb{Z}}u^{i}g_{i}\;\middle|\;g_{i}\in G^{k-2i}\text{ for all }i\in\mathbb{Z}\right\}.

Furthermore, we can naturally identify C^k\hat{C}^{k} with the direct sum:

(162) C^k≅⨁i∈ℤ⨁p=0k−2​iΩp,n−k+2​i+p​(X).\hat{C}^{k}\cong\bigoplus_{i\in\mathbb{Z}}\bigoplus_{p=0}^{k-2i}\Omega^{p,n-k+2i+p}(X).

With this structure in place, we define the following four cohomologies:

  1. (1)

    ∂∂¯\partial\overline{\partial} Cohomology:

    (163) Hsk​(C^∙):=Ker(∂∂¯:C^k⟶C^k)Im(∂∂¯:C^k⟶C^k)H_{s}^{k}(\hat{C}^{\bullet}):=\frac{\mathrm{Ker}(\partial\overline{\partial}:\hat{C}^{k}\longrightarrow\hat{C}^{k})}{\mathrm{Im}(\partial\overline{\partial}:\hat{C}^{k}\longrightarrow\hat{C}^{k})}
  2. (2)

    Periodic Cyclic Cohomology (P.C.):

    (164) H​Pk​(C^∙):=Ker(∂+u∂¯:C^k⟶C^k+1)Im(∂+u∂¯:C^k−1⟶C^k)HP^{k}(\hat{C}^{\bullet}):=\frac{\mathrm{Ker}(\partial+u\overline{\partial}:\hat{C}^{k}\longrightarrow\hat{C}^{k+1})}{\mathrm{Im}(\partial+u\overline{\partial}:\hat{C}^{k-1}\longrightarrow\hat{C}^{k})}
  3. (3)

    Bott-Chern Cohomology:

    (165) H∂+∂¯k​(C^∙):=Ker(∂+u∂¯:C^k⟶C^k+1)Im(∂∂¯:C^k⟶C^k)H_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}^{k}(\hat{C}^{\bullet}):=\frac{\mathrm{Ker}(\partial+u\overline{\partial}:\hat{C}^{k}\longrightarrow\hat{C}^{k+1})}{\mathrm{Im}(\partial\overline{\partial}:\hat{C}^{k}\longrightarrow\hat{C}^{k})}
  4. (4)

    Aeppli Cohomology:

    (166) H∂+∂¯k​(C^∙):=Ker(∂∂¯:C^k⟶C^k)Im(∂+u∂¯:C^k−1⟶C^k)H_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}^{k}(\hat{C}^{\bullet}):=\frac{\mathrm{Ker}(\partial\overline{\partial}:\hat{C}^{k}\longrightarrow\hat{C}^{k})}{\mathrm{Im}(\partial+u\overline{\partial}:\hat{C}^{k-1}\longrightarrow\hat{C}^{k})}

The proof of Theorem 4.3 is analogous to that of Theorem 4.2 and is therefore omitted.

Theorem 4.8.  Let XX be a complex manifold of dimension 2​n2n. There exist subspaces Q^even\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{even}} and Q^odd\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{odd}} in H∂+∂¯k​(C^∙)H_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}^{k}(\hat{C}^{\bullet}) such that, when n≡0(mod2)n\equiv 0\pmod{2},

(167) H∂+∂¯k​(C^∙)≅{(⨁m​ even⨁p+q=mH∂+∂¯p,q​(X))⊕Q^even,k​ even(⨁m​ odd⨁p+q=mH∂+∂¯p,q​(X))⊕Q^odd,k​ oddH_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}^{k}(\hat{C}^{\bullet})\cong\begin{cases}\left(\bigoplus_{m\text{ even}}\bigoplus_{p+q=m}H_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}^{p,q}(X)\right)\oplus\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{even}},&k\text{ even}\\[8.61108pt] \left(\bigoplus_{m\text{ odd}}\bigoplus_{p+q=m}H_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}^{p,q}(X)\right)\oplus\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{odd}},&k\text{ odd}\end{cases}

and when n≡1(mod2)n\equiv 1\pmod{2},

(168) H∂+∂¯k​(C^∙)≅{(⨁m​ odd⨁p+q=mH∂+∂¯p,q​(X))⊕Q^even,k​ even(⨁m​ even⨁p+q=mH∂+∂¯p,q​(X))⊕Q^odd,k​ oddH_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}^{k}(\hat{C}^{\bullet})\cong\begin{cases}\left(\bigoplus_{m\text{ odd}}\bigoplus_{p+q=m}H_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}^{p,q}(X)\right)\oplus\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{even}},&k\text{ even}\\[8.61108pt] \left(\bigoplus_{m\text{ even}}\bigoplus_{p+q=m}H_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}^{p,q}(X)\right)\oplus\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{odd}},&k\text{ odd}\end{cases}

Not surprisingly, akin to Hd+dΛ​(C∙)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(C^{\bullet}), the cohomology H∂+∂¯​(C^∙)H_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}(\hat{C}^{\bullet}) is generically infinite-dimensional:

Example 4.9.  Let XX be a complex manifold of complex dimension 11. Then:

(169) H∂+∂¯odd​(C^∙)\displaystyle H_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}^{\mathrm{odd}}(\hat{C}^{\bullet}) ≃H∂+∂¯1,1​(X)⊕H∂+∂¯0,0​(X)\displaystyle\simeq H^{1,1}_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}(X)\oplus H^{0,0}_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}(X)
(170) H∂+∂¯even​(C^∙)\displaystyle H^{\mathrm{even}}_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}(\hat{C}^{\bullet}) ≃Ker(d:Ω1(X)→Ω2(X)).\displaystyle\simeq\mathrm{Ker}(\mathrm{d}\colon\Omega^{1}(X)\to\Omega^{2}(X)).

4.4. Cohomologies and Non-Kähler SYZ

Given a non-Kähler SYZ mirror pair (X,Ω,ω)(X,\mathsf{\Omega},\omega) and (X˘,ω˘,Ω˘)(\breve{X},\breve{\omega},\breve{\mathsf{\Omega}}), the cohomology Hd+dΛ​(X˘)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X}) measures well-behaved supersymmetric deformations of type IIA SU​(n)\mathrm{SU}(n)-structures [Gen_cohom]. The Fourier-Mukai transform of differential forms yields an isomorphism:

(171) F​T:⨁p=0kΩBp,n−k+p​(X)​⟶≃​ΩBk​(X˘,ℂ).FT\colon\bigoplus^{k}_{p=0}\Omega_{B}^{p,n-k+p}(X)\overset{\simeq}{\longrightarrow}\Omega^{k}_{B}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}).

Lau, Tseng, and Yau established the following equivalence:

Theorem 4.10 (Lau, Tseng, Yau [Lau_2015, Theorem 4.5]).  The Fourier-Mukai transform gives an isomorphism of bicomplexes:

(172) (ΩB​(X˘,ℂ),(−1)n​𝐢2​d,(−1)n​𝐢2​dΛ)≅(ΩB​(X),∂,∂¯).\left(\Omega_{B}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}}),\frac{(-1)^{n}\mathbf{i}}{2}\mathrm{d},\frac{(-1)^{n}\mathbf{i}}{2}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}\right)\cong\left(\Omega_{B}(X),\partial,\overline{\partial}\right).

Moreover, they prove:

Theorem 4.11 (Lau, Tseng, Yau [Lau_2015, Theorem 6.7]).  Given a non-Kähler SYZ mirror pair (X,Ω,ω)(X,\mathsf{\Omega},\omega) and (X˘,ω˘,Ω˘)(\breve{X},\breve{\omega},\breve{\mathsf{\Omega}}), the Fourier-Mukai transform yields an isomorphism:

(173) HB,d+dΛ(n−p,q)Δ​(X˘,ℂ)≃HB,∂+∂¯p,q​(X),H^{(n-p,q)^{\Delta}}_{B,\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X},{\mathbb{C}})\simeq H^{p,q}_{B,\partial+\overline{\partial}}(X),

where Δ\Delta is the real polarization on X˘\breve{X} induced by the bundle structure and

  • •
    (174) HB,∂+∂¯p,q​(X):=Ker​(∂+∂¯)∩ΩBp,q​(X)Im​(∂∂¯)∩ΩBp,q​(X).H^{p,q}_{B,\partial+\overline{\partial}}(X):=\frac{\mathrm{Ker}(\partial+\overline{\partial})\cap\Omega^{p,q}_{B}(X)}{\mathrm{Im}(\partial\overline{\partial})\cap\Omega^{p,q}_{B}(X)}.

    is the basic Bott-Chern cohomology, and

  • •
    (175) HB,d+dΛ(p,q)Δ​(X˘):=Ker​(d+dΛ)∩ΩB(p,q)Δ​(X˘)Im​(ddΛ)∩ΩB(p,q)Δ​(X˘).H_{B,\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{(p,q)^{\Delta}}(\breve{X}):=\frac{\mathrm{Ker}(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda})\cap\Omega^{(p,q)^{\Delta}}_{B}(\breve{X})}{\mathrm{Im}(\mathrm{d}\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda})\cap\Omega^{(p,q)^{\Delta}}_{B}(\breve{X})}.

    is the Δ\Delta-refined Tseng-Yau cohomology.

Although cohomologies Hd+dΛ​(CX˘∙)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(C^{\bullet}_{\breve{X}}) and H∂+∂¯​(C^X∙)H_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}(\hat{C}^{\bullet}_{X}) are generally infinite-dimensional, as a striaghtforward consequence of Theorem 4.4, we have:

Theorem 4.12.  The Fourier-Mukai transform induces the following isomorphism for all kk:

(176) HB,∂+∂¯k​(C^X∙)≅HB,d+dΛk​(CX˘∙).H_{B,\partial+\overline{\partial}}^{k}(\hat{C}_{X}^{\bullet})\cong H^{k}_{B,\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(C_{\breve{X}}^{\bullet}).

5. The Tseng-Yau cohomology of Solvmanifolds

Consider the semi-direct product G⋉ρ∗ℝnG\ltimes_{\rho^{*}}{\mathbb{R}}^{n}, and let {e1,…,e2​n}\{e_{1},\ldots,e_{2n}\} be a basis of left-invariant 11-forms for the Lie algebra 𝔤⋉de​ρ∗ℝn\mathfrak{g}\ltimes_{\mathrm{d}_{e}\rho^{*}}{\mathbb{R}}^{n}. Then the exterior derivatives are given by:

(177) d​ei\displaystyle\mathrm{d}e_{i} =−∑1≤j<k≤nCj,ki​ej∧ek,\displaystyle=-\sum_{1\leq j<k\leq n}C^{i}_{j,k}\,e_{j}\wedge e_{k}, (1≤i≤n),\displaystyle(1\leq i\leq n),
(178) d​ei+n\displaystyle\mathrm{d}e_{i+n} =−∑j,k=1n(de​ρ∗​(Xj))i,k​ej∧ek+n,\displaystyle=-\sum_{j,k=1}^{n}(\mathrm{d}_{e}\rho^{\ast}(X_{j}))_{i,k}\,e_{j}\wedge e_{k+n}, (1≤i≤n).\displaystyle(1\leq i\leq n).

The construction of the non-Kähler SYZ mirror pair in Section 2.3 yields a symplectic manifold (X˘,ω˘,Ω˘)(\breve{X},\breve{\omega},\breve{\mathsf{\Omega}}), which is a solvmanifold obtained as the quotient of G⋉ρ∗ℝnG\ltimes_{\rho^{*}}{\mathbb{R}}^{n} by the lattice Γ⋉ρ∗Ξ∗\Gamma\ltimes_{\rho^{*}}\Xi^{*}. From the definition of the product in G⋉ρ∗ℝnG\ltimes_{\rho^{*}}{\mathbb{R}}^{n}, the cohomology classes in Hd+dΛ​(X˘)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X}) can be represented by left-invariant forms, and the (p,q)(p,q)-decomposition of Hd+dΛ​(X˘)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X}) can be computed explicitly from this basis of left-invariant 11-forms. Theorem 5 below provides a method to compute the cohomology Hd+dΛ​(X˘)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X}). Applying the Fourier-Mukai transform, one can then compute the corresponding cohomology on the mirror manifold XX.

Theorem 5.1 (Angella, Kasuya [AngellaKasuya2019, Theorem 3.2]).  Let X˘=Γ\G\breve{X}=\Gamma\backslash G be a solvmanifold endowed with a GG-left-invariant symplectic structure ω˘\breve{\omega}. Suppose that the natural inclusion map

(179) HdR∙​(𝔤;ℝ)⟶HdR∙​(X˘;ℝ)H^{\bullet}_{\mathrm{dR}}(\mathfrak{g};{\mathbb{R}})\longrightarrow H^{\bullet}_{\mathrm{dR}}(\breve{X};{\mathbb{R}})

is an isomorphism. Then, the natural inclusion map

(180) ι:Hd+dΛ∙​(𝔤;ℝ)⟶Hd+dΛ∙​(X˘;ℝ)\iota\colon H^{\bullet}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\mathfrak{g};{\mathbb{R}})\longrightarrow H^{\bullet}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\breve{X};{\mathbb{R}})

is also an isomorphism.

In this section, we illustrate this result in two distinct cases. For almost abelian manifolds, we provide an explicit description of their Tseng-Yau cohomology. In the case of the generalized Heisenberg group, the computation is more involved and we only present the necessary components for the complete calculation.

5.1. Almost abelian manifolds

Let G=ℝ⋉ex1​Aℝn−1G={\mathbb{R}}\ltimes_{e^{x_{1}A}}{\mathbb{R}}^{n-1} be an almost abelian Lie group, where tr​(A)=0\mathrm{tr}(A)=0. Assume that there exists t0∈ℝ∖{0}t_{0}\in{\mathbb{R}}\setminus\{0\} such that et0​Ae^{t_{0}A} is conjugate to an integer matrix. Consider GG equipped with a complete left-invariant affine structure, given by the affine representation α:G→Aff​(ℝn)\alpha\colon G\to\mathrm{Aff}({\mathbb{R}}^{n}) with linear part ρ​(x1,…,xn)=diag​(1,ex1​A)\rho(x_{1},\ldots,x_{n})=\mathrm{diag}(1,e^{x_{1}A}). As shown in Appendix A, there exists a lattice Γ\Gamma such that the quotient B=G/ΓB=G/\Gamma has an integral affine structure.

Let (X,Ω,ω)→B(X,\mathsf{\Omega},\omega)\to B and (X˘,ω˘,Ω˘)→B(\breve{X},\breve{\omega},\breve{\mathsf{\Omega}})\to B be a supersymmetric mirror pair of type IIB and IIA, respectively, as provided in Theorem 3.2. By Theorem 3.2, the group G⋉ρ∗ℝnG\ltimes_{\rho^{*}}{\mathbb{R}}^{n} acts transitively on X˘\breve{X} with stabilizer Γ⋉ρ∗Ξ\Gamma\ltimes_{\rho^{*}}\Xi. Note that, by the definition of ρ∗\rho^{*}, the quotient (G⋉ρ∗ℝn)/(Γ⋉ρ∗Ξ)(G\ltimes_{\rho^{*}}{\mathbb{R}}^{n})/(\Gamma\ltimes_{\rho^{*}}\Xi) is a solvmanifold. We now proceed to compute the cohomology Hd+dΛ​(𝔤⋉de​ρ∗ℝn;ℝ)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\mathfrak{g}\ltimes_{\mathrm{d}_{e}\rho^{*}}{\mathbb{R}}^{n};{\mathbb{R}}).

Let {e˘1,…,e˘2​n}\{\breve{e}_{1},\ldots,\breve{e}_{2n}\} be a basis of left-invariant 11-forms. The differentials are determined by:

(181) d​e˘i\displaystyle\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{i} =−∑k=2nak​i​e˘1∧e˘k,\displaystyle=-\sum_{k=2}^{n}a_{ki}\breve{e}_{1}\wedge\breve{e}_{k}, (1≤i≤n),\displaystyle(1\leq i\leq n),
(182) d​e˘i+n\displaystyle\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{i+n} =∑k=2nai​k​e˘1∧e˘i+n,\displaystyle=\sum_{k=2}^{n}a_{ik}\breve{e}_{1}\wedge\breve{e}_{i+n}, (1≤i≤n),\displaystyle(1\leq i\leq n),

where d​e˘1=d​e˘n+1=0\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{1}=\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{n+1}=0. Consider the form e˘I∧e˘J+n∈⋀s(𝔤⋉de​ρ∗ℝn)\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+n}\in\bigwedge^{s}\left(\mathfrak{g}\ltimes_{\mathrm{d}_{e}\rho^{*}}{\mathbb{R}}^{n}\right), where I=(i1,…,im)I=(i_{1},\ldots,i_{m}) and J=(j1,…,jr)J=(j_{1},\ldots,j_{r}) are index sets such that m+r=sm+r=s. Expanding the differential, we find:

(183) d​(e˘I∧e˘J+n)\displaystyle\mathrm{d}(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+n}) =−∑l=1m∑k=2nail​k​e˘1∧e˘k∧e˘i1∧⋯∧e˘il^∧⋯∧e˘im∧e˘j1+n∧⋯∧e˘jr+n\displaystyle=-\sum_{l=1}^{m}\sum^{n}_{k=2}a_{i_{l}k}\breve{e}_{1}\wedge\breve{e}_{k}\wedge\breve{e}_{i_{1}}\wedge\dots\wedge\widehat{\breve{e}_{i_{l}}}\wedge\dots\wedge\breve{e}_{i_{m}}\wedge\breve{e}_{j_{1}+n}\wedge\dots\wedge\breve{e}_{j_{r}+n}
(184) +∑l=1r∑k=2nak​jl​e˘1∧e˘i1∧⋯∧e˘im∧e˘k+n∧e˘j1+n∧⋯∧e˘jl+n^∧⋯∧e˘jr+n\displaystyle\quad+\sum^{r}_{l=1}\sum^{n}_{k=2}a_{kj_{l}}\breve{e}_{1}\wedge\breve{e}_{i_{1}}\wedge\dots\wedge\breve{e}_{i_{m}}\wedge\breve{e}_{k+n}\wedge\breve{e}_{j_{1}+n}\wedge\dots\wedge\widehat{\breve{e}_{j_{l}+n}}\wedge\dots\wedge\breve{e}_{j_{r}+n}
(185) =−∑l=1m∑k∈(I∪{1})cail​k​e˘1∧e˘k∧e˘I∖{il}∧e˘J+n\displaystyle=-\sum^{m}_{l=1}\sum_{k\in(I\cup\{1\})^{c}}a_{i_{l}k}\breve{e}_{1}\wedge\breve{e}_{k}\wedge\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{i_{l}\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+n}
(186) +∑l=1r∑k∈(J∪{1})cak​jl​e˘1∧e˘I∧e˘k+n∧e˘J∖{jl}+n\displaystyle\quad+\sum^{r}_{l=1}\sum_{k\in(J\cup\{1\})^{c}}a_{kj_{l}}\breve{e}_{1}\wedge\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{k+n}\wedge\breve{e}_{J\setminus\{j_{l}\}+n}
(187) +(−∑i∈I∖{1}ai​i+∑j∈J∖{1}aj​j)​e˘1∧e˘I∧e˘J+n.\displaystyle\quad+\left(-\sum_{i\in I\setminus\{1\}}a_{ii}+\sum_{j\in J\setminus\{1\}}a_{jj}\right)\breve{e}_{1}\wedge\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+n}.

Consequently, if 1∉I1\notin I, the condition d​(e˘I∧e˘J+n)=0\mathrm{d}(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+n})=0 is satisfied if and only if:

(188) ai​k\displaystyle a_{ik} =0for all ​i∈I,k∈(I∪{1})c,\displaystyle=0\quad\text{for all }i\in I,\;k\in(I\cup\{1\})^{c},
(189) ak​j\displaystyle a_{kj} =0for all ​j∈J,k∈(J∪{1})c,\displaystyle=0\quad\text{for all }j\in J,\;k\in(J\cup\{1\})^{c},
(190) ∑i∈Iai​i\displaystyle\sum_{i\in I}a_{ii} =∑j∈J∖{1}aj​j.\displaystyle=\sum_{j\in J\setminus\{1\}}a_{jj}.

If 1∈I1\in I, the differential vanishes identically, i.e., d​(e˘I∧e˘J+n)=0\mathrm{d}(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+n})=0.

Recalling that ω˘=∑k=1ne˘k∧e˘k+n\breve{\omega}=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\breve{e}_{k}\wedge\breve{e}_{k+n}, the dual Lefschetz operator acts as:

(191) Λ​(e˘I∧e˘J+n)=∑σ∈I∩J(−1)ξσ​e˘I∖{σ}∧e˘J∖{σ}+n\Lambda(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+n})=\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap J}(-1)^{\xi_{\sigma}}\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{J\setminus\{\sigma\}+n}

for appropriate signs (−1)ξσ(-1)^{\xi_{\sigma}}. Differentiating this yields:

(192) d​Λ​(e˘I∧e˘J+n)\displaystyle\mathrm{d}\Lambda(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+n}) =∑σ∈I∩J(−1)ξσ​d​(e˘I∖{σ}∧e˘J∖{σ}+n)\displaystyle=\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap J}(-1)^{\xi_{\sigma}}\mathrm{d}\left(\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{J\setminus\{\sigma\}+n}\right)
(193) =−∑σ∈I∩J∑i∈I∖{σ}∑k∈(I∖{σ,1})c(−1)ξσ​ai​k​e˘1∧e˘k∧e˘I∖{i,σ}∧e˘J∖{σ}+n\displaystyle=-\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap J}\sum_{i\in I\setminus\{\sigma\}}\sum_{k\in(I\setminus\{\sigma,1\})^{c}}(-1)^{\xi_{\sigma}}a_{ik}\breve{e}_{1}\wedge\breve{e}_{k}\wedge\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{i,\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{J\setminus\{\sigma\}+n}
(194) +∑σ∈I∩J∑j∈J∖{σ}∑k∈(J∖{σ,1})c(−1)ξσ​ak​j​e˘1∧e˘I∖{σ}∧e˘k+n∧e˘J∖{j,σ}+n\displaystyle\quad+\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap J}\sum_{j\in J\setminus\{\sigma\}}\sum_{k\in(J\setminus\{\sigma,1\})^{c}}(-1)^{\xi_{\sigma}}a_{kj}\breve{e}_{1}\wedge\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{k+n}\wedge\breve{e}_{J\setminus\{j,\sigma\}+n}
(195) +∑σ∈I∩J(−1)ξσ​(−∑i∈I∖{σ,1}ai​i+∑j∈J∖{σ,1}aj​j)​e˘1∧e˘I∖{σ}∧e˘J∖{σ}+n.\displaystyle\quad+\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap J}(-1)^{\xi_{\sigma}}\left(-\sum_{i\in I\setminus\{\sigma,1\}}a_{ii}+\sum_{j\in J\setminus\{\sigma,1\}}a_{jj}\right)\breve{e}_{1}\wedge\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{J\setminus\{\sigma\}+n}.

For 1∈I1\in I and 1∈J1\in J, the vanishing condition d​Λ​(e˘I∧e˘J+n)=0\mathrm{d}\Lambda(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+n})=0 simplifies to:

(196) ai​k\displaystyle a_{ik} =0for all ​i∈I∖{1},k∈(I∖{1})c,\displaystyle=0\quad\text{for all }i\in I\setminus\{1\},\;k\in(I\setminus\{1\})^{c},
(197) ak​j\displaystyle a_{kj} =0for all ​j∈J∖{1},k∈(J∖{1})c,\displaystyle=0\quad\text{for all }j\in J\setminus\{1\},\;k\in(J\setminus\{1\})^{c},
(198) ∑i∈I∖{1}ai​i\displaystyle\sum_{i\in I\setminus\{1\}}a_{ii} =∑j∈J∖{1}aj​j.\displaystyle=\sum_{j\in J\setminus\{1\}}a_{jj}.

We explicitly characterize the kernel of the differential as follows:

(199) ker⁡(d)\displaystyle\ker(\mathrm{d}) ={e˘I∧e˘J+n|I⊂{2,…,n},J⊂{1,…,n},|I|+|J|=k,ai​k=0​ for ​i∈I,k∈(I∪{1})c,ak​j=0​ for ​j∈J,k∈(J∪{1})c,∑i∈Iai​i=∑j∈Jaj​j}\displaystyle=\left\{\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+n}\;\middle|\;\begin{aligned} &I\subset\{2,\ldots,n\},\,J\subset\{1,\ldots,n\},\,|I|+|J|=k,\\ &a_{ik}=0\text{ for }i\in I,\;k\in(I\cup\{1\})^{c},\\ &a_{kj}=0\text{ for }j\in J,\;k\in(J\cup\{1\})^{c},\\ &\sum_{i\in I}a_{ii}=\sum_{j\in J}a_{jj}\end{aligned}\right\}
(200) ∪⟨e˘1∧e˘I∧e˘J+n|I⊂{2,…,n},J⊂{1,…,n},|I|+|J|=k−1⟩.\displaystyle\quad\cup\left\langle\breve{e}_{1}\wedge\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+n}\;\middle|\;\begin{aligned} &I\subset\{2,\ldots,n\},\,J\subset\{1,\ldots,n\},\\ &|I|+|J|=k-1\end{aligned}\right\rangle.

To compute the cohomology, we assume that AA is a diagonal matrix. Under this assumption, for 1∈J1\in J, the operator acts as:

(201) d​Λ​d​(e˘I∧e˘J+n)\displaystyle\mathrm{d}\Lambda\mathrm{d}(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+n}) =(−∑i∈Iai​i+∑j∈Jaj​j)​d​(e˘I∧e˘J∖{1}+n)\displaystyle=\left(-\sum_{i\in I}a_{ii}+\sum_{j\in J}a_{jj}\right)\mathrm{d}\left(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J\setminus\{1\}+n}\right)
(202) =(−∑i∈Iai​i+∑j∈Jaj​j)2​e˘1∧e˘I∧e˘J∖{1}+n.\displaystyle=\left(-\sum_{i\in I}a_{ii}+\sum_{j\in J}a_{jj}\right)^{2}\breve{e}_{1}\wedge\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J\setminus\{1\}+n}.

Recalling that Hd+dΛk​(X˘)=ker⁡(d)∩ker⁡(d​Λ)Im⁡(d​Λ​d)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{k}(\breve{X})=\frac{\ker(\mathrm{d})\cap\ker(\mathrm{d}\Lambda)}{\operatorname{Im}(\mathrm{d}\Lambda\mathrm{d})}, we arrive at the following explicit characterization of the cohomology:

(203) Hd+dΛk​(X˘)≅⟨e˘I∧e˘J+n|I,J⊂{1,…,n},|I|+|J|=k∑i∈I∖{1}ai​i=∑j∈J∖{1}aj​j⟩.H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}^{k}(\breve{X})\cong\left\langle\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+n}\;\middle|\;\begin{aligned} &I,J\subset\{1,\ldots,n\},\,|I|+|J|=k\\ &\sum_{i\in I\setminus\{1\}}a_{ii}=\sum_{j\in J\setminus\{1\}}a_{jj}\end{aligned}\right\rangle.

The corresponding (p,q)(p,q)-decomposition is then given by:

(204) Hd+dΛ,Bp,q​(X˘)≅⟨e˘I∧e˘J+n|I,J⊂{1,…,n},|I|=p,|J|=q∑i∈I∖{1}ai​i=∑j∈J∖{1}aj​j⟩.H^{p,q}_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda},B}(\breve{X})\cong\left\langle\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+n}\;\middle|\;\begin{aligned} &I,J\subset\{1,\ldots,n\},\,|I|=p,\,|J|=q\\ &\sum_{i\in I\setminus\{1\}}a_{ii}=\sum_{j\in J\setminus\{1\}}a_{jj}\end{aligned}\right\rangle.

Finally, by Theorem 4.4, the Bott-Chern cohomology on the complex mirror side XX is:

(205) H∂+∂¯,Bp,q​(X)≅⟨eI∧e¯J|I,J⊂{1,…,n},|I|=n−p,|J|=q∑i∈I∖{1}ai​i=∑j∈J∖{1}aj​j⟩ℂ.H^{p,q}_{\partial+\overline{\partial},B}(X)\cong\left\langle e_{I}\wedge\overline{e}_{J}\;\middle|\;\begin{aligned} &I,J\subset\{1,\ldots,n\},\,|I|=n-p,\,|J|=q\\ &\sum_{i\in I\setminus\{1\}}a_{ii}=\sum_{j\in J\setminus\{1\}}a_{jj}\end{aligned}\right\rangle_{{\mathbb{C}}}.

Remark 5.2.  It is possible to compute the cohomology on the complex side explicitly. This is the content of Theorem 5.1, whose proof is omitted:

Theorem 5.3.  Let G=ℝ⋉ex1​Aℝn−1G={\mathbb{R}}\ltimes_{e^{x_{1}A}}{\mathbb{R}}^{n-1} be an almost abelian Lie group, where AA is a diagonalizable matrix with tr​(A)=0\mathrm{tr}(A)=0. Let ρ:G→GL​(n,ℝ)\rho\colon G\to\mathrm{GL}(n,{\mathbb{R}}) be such that ρ​(x1,…,xn)=diag​(1,ex1​A)\rho(x_{1},\ldots,x_{n})=\mathrm{diag}(1,e^{x_{1}A}). Assume the existence of a lattice Γ⋉ρΞ\Gamma\ltimes_{\rho}\Xi such that (G⋉ρℝn)/(Γ⋉ρΞ)(G\ltimes_{\rho}{\mathbb{R}}^{n})/(\Gamma\ltimes_{\rho}\Xi) is a solvmanifold equipped with the canonical left-invariant complex structure. Then

(206) H∂+∂¯p,q​(X)≅spanℂ​{YI∗∧YJ∗¯||I|=p,|J|=q∑k∈I∖{1}λk=−∑j∈J∖{1}λj}H^{p,q}_{\partial+\overline{\partial}}(X)\cong\mathrm{span}_{{\mathbb{C}}}\left\{Y^{*}_{I}\wedge\overline{Y^{*}_{J}}\;\middle|\;\begin{aligned} &|I|=p,\,|J|=q\\ &\sum_{k\in I\setminus\{1\}}\lambda_{k}=-\sum_{j\in J\setminus\{1\}}\lambda_{j}\end{aligned}\right\}

where λ2,…,λn\lambda_{2},\ldots,\lambda_{n} are the eigenvalues of AA; Y2,…,YnY_{2},\ldots,Y_{n} are the associated eigenvectors and d​z1=Y1∗\mathrm{d}z_{1}=Y^{*}_{1}.

5.2. Generalized Heisenberg group

Let ℋ2​n+1​(ℝ)\mathcal{H}_{2n+1}({\mathbb{R}}) be the generalized Heisenberg group. It admits a left-invariant affine structure given by the developing map:

(207) D:ℋ2​n+1​(ℝ)\displaystyle D\colon\mathcal{H}_{2n+1}({\mathbb{R}}) ⟶ℝ2​n+1\displaystyle\longrightarrow{\mathbb{R}}^{2n+1}
(208) [1a¯c0Inb¯001]\displaystyle\begin{bmatrix}1&\overline{a}&c\\ 0&I_{n}&\overline{b}\\ 0&0&1\end{bmatrix} ⟼(a¯,c,b¯).\displaystyle\longmapsto(\overline{a},c,\overline{b}).

Furthermore, ℋ2​n+1​(ℝ)\mathcal{H}_{2n+1}({\mathbb{R}}) contains a lattice Γ\Gamma such that the affine structure induced on the quotient ℋ2​n+1​(ℝ)/Γ\mathcal{H}_{2n+1}({\mathbb{R}})/\Gamma is integral. The linear part of the affine representation is explicitly given by:

(209) ρ:ℋ2​n+1​(ℝ)\displaystyle\rho\colon\mathcal{H}_{2n+1}({\mathbb{R}}) ⟶GL​(ℝ2​n+1)\displaystyle\longrightarrow\mathrm{GL}({\mathbb{R}}^{2n+1})
(210) [1a¯c0Inb¯001]\displaystyle\begin{bmatrix}1&\overline{a}&c\\ 0&I_{n}&\overline{b}\\ 0&0&1\end{bmatrix} ⟼[In0001a¯00In].\displaystyle\longmapsto\begin{bmatrix}I_{n}&0&0\\ 0&1&\overline{a}\\ 0&0&I_{n}\end{bmatrix}.

Let {X1,…,Xn,T,Y1,…,Yn}\{X_{1},\ldots,X_{n},T,Y_{1},\ldots,Y_{n}\} be a basis for the Lie algebra of ℋ2​n+1​(ℝ)\mathcal{H}_{2n+1}({\mathbb{R}}). We then have:

(211) ρ∗​(Xi)=[0n0000Ei000n]=En+1,n+1+i\rho_{*}(X_{i})=\begin{bmatrix}0_{n}&0&0\\ 0&0&E_{i}\\ 0&0&0_{n}\end{bmatrix}=E_{n+1,n+1+i}

where EiE_{i} is a row vector of zeros with a 11 in the ii-th entry, and Ei,jE_{i,j} denotes a matrix with a 11 in the (i,j)(i,j)-th entry and zeros everywhere else. Additionally, ρ∗​(Yi)=ρ∗​(T)=0\rho_{*}(Y_{i})=\rho_{*}(T)=0.

Denoting by ρ∗\rho^{*} the dual representation of ρ\rho, we find:

(212) de​ρ∗​(Xi)=−(de​ρ​(Xi))T\mathrm{d}_{e}\rho^{*}(X_{i})=-(\mathrm{d}_{e}\rho(X_{i}))^{T}

and de​ρ∗​(Yi)=de​ρ∗​(T)=0\mathrm{d}_{e}\rho^{*}(Y_{i})=\mathrm{d}_{e}\rho^{*}(T)=0 for all 1≤i≤n1\leq i\leq n. By Lemma 3.2, the structure constants of G⋉ρ∗ℝnG\ltimes_{\rho^{*}}{\mathbb{R}}^{n} are given by:

(213) cq​rp={Cq​rp,1≤p,q,r≤2​n+1,−(ρ∗​(Sq))r−n,p−n,1≤q≤2​n+1, 2​n+2≤p,r≤4​n+2,(ρ∗​(Sr))q−n,p−n,2​n+2≤q≤4​n+2, 1≤r≤2​n+1, 2​n+2≤p≤4​n+2,0,otherwise,c^{p}_{qr}=\begin{cases}C^{p}_{qr},&1\leq p,q,r\leq 2n+1,\\[4.0pt] -(\rho_{\ast}(S_{q}))_{r-n,\,p-n},&1\leq q\leq 2n+1,\;2n+2\leq p,r\leq 4n+2,\\[4.0pt] (\rho_{\ast}(S_{r}))_{q-n,\,p-n},&2n+2\leq q\leq 4n+2,\;1\leq r\leq 2n+1,\;2n+2\leq p\leq 4n+2,\\[4.0pt] 0,&\text{otherwise},\end{cases}

where {S1,…,S2​n+1}\{S_{1},\ldots,S_{2n+1}\} is a basis for 𝔥\mathfrak{h}, defined as Si=XiS_{i}=X_{i} for 1≤i≤n1\leq i\leq n, Sn+1=TS_{n+1}=T, and Sn+1+i=YiS_{n+1+i}=Y_{i} for 1≤i≤n1\leq i\leq n. Because adX\mathrm{ad}_{X} has real eigenvalues for all XX, the semi-direct product G⋉ρ∗ℝnG\ltimes_{\rho^{*}}{\mathbb{R}}^{n} is completely solvable.

Let (X,Ω,ω)→B(X,\mathsf{\Omega},\omega)\to B and (X˘,ω˘,Ω˘)→B(\breve{X},\breve{\omega},\breve{\mathsf{\Omega}})\to B be a supersymmetric mirror pair of type IIB and IIA, respectively, as provided in Theorem 3.2. By Theorem 3.2, G⋉ρ∗ℝnG\ltimes_{\rho^{*}}{\mathbb{R}}^{n} acts transitively on X˘\breve{X} with stabilizer Γ⋉ρ∗Ξ\Gamma\ltimes_{\rho^{*}}\Xi. Note that, by the definition of ρ∗\rho^{*}, the quotient (G⋉ρ∗ℝn)/(Γ⋉ρ∗Ξ)(G\ltimes_{\rho^{*}}{\mathbb{R}}^{n})/(\Gamma\ltimes_{\rho^{*}}\Xi) is a solvmanifold.

Our goal is to compute Hd+dΛ​(𝔤⋉de​ρ∗ℝn;ℝ)H_{\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{d}^{\Lambda}}(\mathfrak{g}\ltimes_{\mathrm{d}_{e}\rho^{*}}{\mathbb{R}}^{n};{\mathbb{R}}). Let {e˘1,…,e˘4​n+2}\{\breve{e}_{1},\ldots,\breve{e}_{4n+2}\} be a basis of left-invariant 11-forms. The structure constants of ℋ2​n+1​(ℝ)\mathcal{H}_{2n+1}({\mathbb{R}}) dictate:

(214) Cj,ki={1,i=n+1, 1≤j≤n,k=j+n+1,−1,i=n+1, 1≤k≤n,j=k+n+1,0,otherwise.C^{i}_{j,k}=\begin{cases}1,&i=n+1,\,1\leq j\leq n,\,k=j+n+1,\\ -1,&i=n+1,\,1\leq k\leq n,\,j=k+n+1,\\ 0,&\text{otherwise}.\end{cases}

Thus, d​e˘i=d​e˘i+n+1=0\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{i}=\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{i+n+1}=0 for all 1≤i≤n1\leq i\leq n, and:

(215) d​e˘n+1=−∑i=1ne˘i∧e˘i+n+1.\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{n+1}=-\sum_{i=1}^{n}\breve{e}_{i}\wedge\breve{e}_{i+n+1}.

Recall that:

(216) d​e˘i+2​n+1=∑j,k=12​n+1(ρ∗​(Sj))k,i​e˘j∧e˘k+2​n+1(1≤i≤2​n+1).\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{i+2n+1}=\sum_{j,k=1}^{2n+1}(\rho_{\ast}(S_{j}))_{k,i}\,\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{k+2n+1}\qquad(1\leq i\leq 2n+1).

This simplifies to:

(217) d​e˘i+2​n+1=∑j=1n∑k=12​n+1(ρ∗​(Xj))k,i​e˘j∧e˘k+2​n+1(1≤i≤2​n+1).\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{i+2n+1}=\sum^{n}_{j=1}\sum_{k=1}^{2n+1}(\rho_{\ast}(X_{j}))_{k,i}\,\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{k+2n+1}\qquad(1\leq i\leq 2n+1).

Noting that (ρ∗​(Xj))k,i=δk,n+1​δi,j+n+1(\rho_{*}(X_{j}))_{k,i}=\delta_{k,n+1}\delta_{i,j+n+1}, we obtain:

(218) d​e˘i+2​n+1=∑j=1n∑k=12​n+1δk,n+1​δi,j+n+1​e˘j∧e˘k+2​n+1=∑j=1nδi,j+n+1​e˘j∧e˘3​n+2(1≤i≤2​n+1).\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{i+2n+1}=\sum^{n}_{j=1}\sum_{k=1}^{2n+1}\delta_{k,n+1}\delta_{i,j+n+1}\,\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{k+2n+1}=\sum^{n}_{j=1}\delta_{i,j+n+1}\,\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}\qquad(1\leq i\leq 2n+1).

Consequently, we have:

(219) d​e˘i+2​n+1\displaystyle\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{i+2n+1} =0,\displaystyle=0, (1≤i≤n+1),\displaystyle(1\leq i\leq n+1),
(220) d​e˘i+3​n+2\displaystyle\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{i+3n+2} =e˘i∧e˘3​n+2,\displaystyle=\breve{e}_{i}\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}, (1≤i≤n).\displaystyle(1\leq i\leq n).

Let ω˘=∑i=12​n+1e˘i∧e˘i+2​n+1\breve{\omega}=\sum_{i=1}^{2n+1}\breve{e}_{i}\wedge\breve{e}_{i+2n+1}. Consider index sets I=(i1<…<im)I=(i_{1}<\ldots<i_{m}) and J=(j1<…<jr)J=(j_{1}<\ldots<j_{r}) with I,J⊂{1,…,2​n+1}I,J\subset\{1,\ldots,2n+1\}. Suppose first that n+1∉In+1\notin I. Then:

(221) d​(e˘I∧e˘J+2​n+1)=(−1)|I|​e˘I∧d​e˘J+2​n+1.\mathrm{d}(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+2n+1})=(-1)^{|I|}\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{J+2n+1}.

Denoting W={1,…,n+1}W=\{1,\ldots,n+1\}, we can expand this as:

(222) d​(e˘I∧e˘J+2​n+1)\displaystyle\mathrm{d}(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+2n+1}) =(−1)|I|+|J∩W|​e˘I∧e˘(J∩W)+2​n+1∧d​e˘(J∖W)+2​n+1\displaystyle=(-1)^{|I|+|J\cap W|}\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cap W)+2n+1}\wedge\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{(J\setminus W)+2n+1}
(223) =∑j∈J∖W(−1)|I|+|J∩W|​e˘I∧e˘(J∩W)+2​n+1∧e˘j∧e˘3​n+2∧e˘(J∖(W∪{j}))+2​n+1\displaystyle=\sum_{j\in J\setminus W}(-1)^{|I|+|J\cap W|}\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cap W)+2n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\setminus(W\cup\{j\}))+2n+1}
(224) =∑j∈J∖We˘j∧e˘I∧e˘(J∩W)+2​n+1∧e˘3​n+2∧e˘(J∖(W∪{j}))+2​n+1\displaystyle=\sum_{j\in J\setminus W}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cap W)+2n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\setminus(W\cup\{j\}))+2n+1}
(225) =∑j∈J∖W∩Ice˘j∧e˘I∧e˘(J∩W)+2​n+1∧e˘3​n+2∧e˘(J∖(W∪{j}))+2​n+1\displaystyle=\sum_{j\in J\setminus W\cap I^{c}}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cap W)+2n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\setminus(W\cup\{j\}))+2n+1}
(226) =∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wce˘j∧e˘I∧e˘(J∩W)+2​n+1∧e˘3​n+2∧e˘(J∖(W∪{j}))+2​n+1.\displaystyle=\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cap W)+2n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\setminus(W\cup\{j\}))+2n+1}.

Suppose instead that n+1∈In+1\in I. We can decompose I=I1∪{n+1}∪(I2+n+1)I=I_{1}\cup\{n+1\}\cup(I_{2}+n+1), where I1,I2⊂{1,…,n}I_{1},I_{2}\subset\{1,\ldots,n\}. This yields:

(227) d​(e˘I∧e˘J+2​n+1)\displaystyle\mathrm{d}(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+2n+1}) =∑i=1ne˘i∧e˘I1∧e˘i+n+1∧e˘I2+n+1∧e˘J+2​n+1\displaystyle=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\breve{e}_{i}\wedge\breve{e}_{I_{1}}\wedge\breve{e}_{i+n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{I_{2}+n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+2n+1}
(228) +∑j∈J∖We˘j∧e˘I∧e˘(J∩W)+2​n+1∧e˘3​n+2∧e˘(J∖(W∪{j}))+2​n+1\displaystyle\quad+\sum_{j\in J\setminus W}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cap W)+2n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\setminus(W\cup\{j\}))+2n+1}
(229) =∑i∈(I1∪I2)ce˘i∧e˘I1∧e˘i+n+1∧e˘I2+n+1∧e˘J+2​n+1\displaystyle=\sum_{i\in(I_{1}\cup I_{2})^{c}}\breve{e}_{i}\wedge\breve{e}_{I_{1}}\wedge\breve{e}_{i+n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{I_{2}+n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+2n+1}
(230) +∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wce˘j∧e˘I∧e˘(J∩W)+2​n+1∧e˘3​n+2∧e˘(J∖(W∪{j}))+2​n+1.\displaystyle\quad+\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cap W)+2n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\setminus(W\cup\{j\}))+2n+1}.

Applying the dual Lefschetz operator Λ\Lambda and taking the exterior derivative yields:

(231) d​Λ​(e˘I∧e˘J+2​n+1)=∑σ∈I∩Jd​(e˘I∖{σ}∧e˘(J∖{σ})+2​n+1).\mathrm{d}\Lambda(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+2n+1})=\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap J}\mathrm{d}\left(\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\setminus\{\sigma\})+2n+1}\right).

If n+1∉In+1\notin I, this expands to:

(232) d​Λ​(e˘I∧e˘J+2​n+1)\displaystyle\mathrm{d}\Lambda(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+2n+1}) =∑σ∈I∩J∑j∈(J∩Ic∩Wc)∖{σ}e˘j∧e˘I∖{σ}∧e˘(J∩W)∖{σ}+2​n+1∧e˘3​n+2∧e˘J∖(W∪{j,σ})+2​n+1\displaystyle=\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap J}\sum_{j\in(J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c})\setminus\{\sigma\}}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cap W)\setminus\{\sigma\}+2n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}\wedge\breve{e}_{J\setminus(W\cup\{j,\sigma\})+2n+1}
(233) =∑σ∈I∩J∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wce˘j∧e˘I∖{σ}∧e˘(J∩W)∖{σ}+2​n+1∧e˘3​n+2∧e˘J∖(W∪{j,σ})+2​n+1.\displaystyle=\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap J}\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cap W)\setminus\{\sigma\}+2n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}\wedge\breve{e}_{J\setminus(W\cup\{j,\sigma\})+2n+1}.

If n+1∈In+1\in I:

(234) d​Λ​(e˘I∧e˘J+2​n+1)\displaystyle\mathrm{d}\Lambda(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+2n+1}) =∑σ∈I∩J∑i∈(I1∪I2∪{σ})ce˘i∧e˘I1∖{σ}∧e˘i+n+1∧e˘(I2∖{σ})+n+1∧e˘(J∖{σ})+2​n+1\displaystyle=\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap J}\sum_{i\in(I_{1}\cup I_{2}\cup\{\sigma\})^{c}}\breve{e}_{i}\wedge\breve{e}_{I_{1}\setminus\{\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{i+n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{(I_{2}\setminus\{\sigma\})+n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\setminus\{\sigma\})+2n+1}
(235) +∑σ∈I∩J∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wce˘j∧e˘I∖{σ}∧e˘(J∩W)∖{σ}+2​n+1∧e˘3​n+2∧e˘J∖(W∪{j,σ})+2​n+1.\displaystyle\quad+\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap J}\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cap W)\setminus\{\sigma\}+2n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}\wedge\breve{e}_{J\setminus(W\cup\{j,\sigma\})+2n+1}.

Furthermore, if n+1∉In+1\notin I, the composition d​Λ​d\mathrm{d}\Lambda\mathrm{d} vanishes:

(236) d​Λ​d​(e˘I∧e˘J+2​n+1)\displaystyle\mathrm{d}\Lambda\mathrm{d}(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+2n+1}) =d​Λ​(∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wce˘j∧e˘I∧e˘(J∩W)+2​n+1∧e˘3​n+2∧e˘(J∖(W∪{j}))+2​n+1)\displaystyle=\mathrm{d}\Lambda\left(\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cap W)+2n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\setminus(W\cup\{j\}))+2n+1}\right)
(237) =d​(∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wc∑σ∈I∩Je˘j∧e˘I∧e˘(J∩W)∖{σ}+2​n+1∧e˘3​n+2∧e˘(J∖(W∪{j,σ}))+2​n+1)\displaystyle=\mathrm{d}\left(\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap J}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cap W)\setminus\{\sigma\}+2n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\setminus(W\cup\{j,\sigma\}))+2n+1}\right)
(238) =∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wc∑σ∈I∩J∑k∈J∩Wc∖{j}∩(Ic∪{σ})e˘k∧e˘j∧e˘I∖{σ}∧e˘(J∩W)∖{σ}+2​n+1\displaystyle=\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap J}\sum_{k\in J\cap W^{c}\setminus\{j\}\cap(I^{c}\cup\{\sigma\})}\breve{e}_{k}\wedge\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cap W)\setminus\{\sigma\}+2n+1}
(239) ∧e˘3​n+2∧e˘3​n+2∧e˘(J∖(W∪{j,σ,k}))+2​n+1\displaystyle\quad\quad\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\setminus(W\cup\{j,\sigma,k\}))+2n+1}
(240) =0.\displaystyle=0.

Finally, if n+1∈In+1\in I and n+1∉Jn+1\notin J, we expand the d​Λ​d\mathrm{d}\Lambda\mathrm{d} operation into three distinct parts:

(241) d​Λ​d\displaystyle\mathrm{d}\Lambda\mathrm{d} (e˘I∧e˘J+2​n+1)\displaystyle(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+2n+1})
(242) =d​Λ​(∑i∈(I1∪I2)ce˘i∧e˘I1∧e˘i+n+1∧e˘I2+n+1∧e˘J+2​n+1)\displaystyle=\mathrm{d}\Lambda\left(\sum_{i\in(I_{1}\cup I_{2})^{c}}\breve{e}_{i}\wedge\breve{e}_{I_{1}}\wedge\breve{e}_{i+n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{I_{2}+n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+2n+1}\right)
(243) +d​Λ​(∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wce˘j∧e˘I∧e˘(J∩W)+2​n+1∧e˘3​n+2∧e˘(J∖(W∪{j}))+2​n+1)\displaystyle\quad+\mathrm{d}\Lambda\Bigg(\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cap W)+2n+1}\wedge\breve{e}_{3n+2}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\setminus(W\cup\{j\}))+2n+1}\Bigg)
(244) =∑i∈(I1∪I2)c∑σ∈(I∪{i,i+n+1})∩Jd​(e˘(I1∪I2+n+1∪{i,i+n+1})∖{σ}∧e˘(J∖{σ})+2​n+1)\displaystyle=\sum_{i\in(I_{1}\cup I_{2})^{c}}\sum_{\sigma\in(I\cup\{i,i+n+1\})\cap J}\mathrm{d}\Bigg(\breve{e}_{(I_{1}\cup I_{2}+n+1\cup\{i,i+n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\setminus\{\sigma\})+2n+1}\Bigg)
(245) +∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wc∑σ∈I∩((J∪{n+1})∖{j})d​(e˘j∧e˘I∖{σ}∧e˘(J∪{n+1})∖{σ,j}+2​n+1)\displaystyle\quad+\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap((J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{j\})}\mathrm{d}\Bigg(\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma,j\}+2n+1}\Bigg)
(246) =∑i∈(I1∪I2)c∑σ∈(I∪{i,i+n+1})∩J∑j∈J∖(W∪{σ})e˘j\displaystyle=\sum_{i\in(I_{1}\cup I_{2})^{c}}\sum_{\sigma\in(I\cup\{i,i+n+1\})\cap J}\sum_{j\in J\setminus(W\cup\{\sigma\})}\breve{e}_{j}
(247) ∧e˘(I1∪I2+n+1∪{i,i+n+1})∖{σ}∧e˘(J∪{n+1})∖{σ,j}+2​n+1\displaystyle\qquad\wedge\breve{e}_{(I_{1}\cup I_{2}+n+1\cup\{i,i+n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma,j\}+2n+1}
(248) +∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wc∑σ∈I∩((J∪{n+1})∖{j})(d​e˘j∧e˘I∖{σ})∧e˘(J∪{n+1})∖{σ,j}+2​n+1\displaystyle\quad+\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap((J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{j\})}\left(\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{\sigma\}}\right)\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma,j\}+2n+1}
(249) +(−1)|I|​∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wc∑σ∈I∩((J∪{n+1})∖{j})e˘j∧e˘I∖{σ}\displaystyle\quad+(-1)^{|I|}\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap((J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{j\})}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{\sigma\}}
(250) ∧d​(e˘(J∪{n+1})∖{σ,j}+2​n+1).\displaystyle\qquad\wedge\mathrm{d}\left(\breve{e}_{(J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma,j\}+2n+1}\right).

We can simplify the final two summations:

(251) ∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wc∑σ∈I∩((J∪{n+1})∖{j})(d​e˘j∧e˘I∖{σ})∧e˘(J∪{n+1})∖{σ,j}+2​n+1\displaystyle\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap((J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{j\})}\left(\mathrm{d}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{\sigma\}}\right)\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma,j\}+2n+1}
(252) +(−1)|I|​∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wc∑σ∈I∩((J∪{n+1})∖{j})e˘j∧e˘I∖{σ}\displaystyle\quad+(-1)^{|I|}\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap((J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{j\})}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{\sigma\}}
(253) ∧d​(e˘(J∪{n+1})∖{σ,j}+2​n+1)\displaystyle\qquad\wedge\mathrm{d}\left(\breve{e}_{(J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma,j\}+2n+1}\right)
(254) =∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wc∑σ∈I∩(J∖{j})∑i=1ne˘(I1∪I2+n+1∪{j,i,i+n+1})∖{σ}\displaystyle=\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap(J\setminus\{j\})}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\breve{e}_{(I_{1}\cup I_{2}+n+1\cup\{j,i,i+n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma\}}
(255) ∧e˘(J∪{n+1})∖{σ,j}+2​n+1\displaystyle\qquad\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma,j\}+2n+1}
(256) +(−1)|I|​∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wce˘j∧e˘I∖{n+1}∧d​(e˘(J∖{j})+2​n+1)\displaystyle\quad+(-1)^{|I|}\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{n+1\}}\wedge\mathrm{d}\left(\breve{e}_{(J\setminus\{j\})+2n+1}\right)
(257) =∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wc∑σ∈I∩(J∖{j})∑i=1ne˘(I1∪I2+n+1∪{j,i,i+n+1})∖{σ}\displaystyle=\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap(J\setminus\{j\})}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\breve{e}_{(I_{1}\cup I_{2}+n+1\cup\{j,i,i+n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma\}}
(258) ∧e˘(J∪{n+1})∖{σ,j}+2​n+1\displaystyle\qquad\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma,j\}+2n+1}
(259) +(−1)|I|​∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wc∑k∈J∖(W∪{j})e˘k∧e˘j∧e˘I∖{n+1}∧e˘(J∪{n+1})∖{j}+2​n+1.\displaystyle\quad+(-1)^{|I|}\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\sum_{k\in J\setminus(W\cup\{j\})}\breve{e}_{k}\wedge\breve{e}_{j}\wedge\breve{e}_{I\setminus\{n+1\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{j\}+2n+1}.

Substituting this back, we arrive at the closed-form expression for n+1∈In+1\in I and n+1∉Jn+1\notin J:

(260) d​Λ​d\displaystyle\mathrm{d}\Lambda\mathrm{d} (e˘I∧e˘J+2​n+1)\displaystyle(\breve{e}_{I}\wedge\breve{e}_{J+2n+1})
(261) =∑i∈(I1∪I2)c∑σ∈(I∪{i,i+n+1})∩J∑j∈J∖(W∪{σ})e˘j\displaystyle=\sum_{i\in(I_{1}\cup I_{2})^{c}}\sum_{\sigma\in(I\cup\{i,i+n+1\})\cap J}\sum_{j\in J\setminus(W\cup\{\sigma\})}\breve{e}_{j}
(262) ∧e˘(I1∪I2+n+1∪{i,i+n+1})∖{σ}∧e˘(J∪{n+1})∖{σ,j}+2​n+1\displaystyle\qquad\wedge\breve{e}_{(I_{1}\cup I_{2}+n+1\cup\{i,i+n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma,j\}+2n+1}
(263) +∑j∈J∩Ic∩Wc∑σ∈I∩(J∖{j})∑i∈(I1∪I2∪{j−(n+1)}∖{σ})c\displaystyle\quad+\sum_{j\in J\cap I^{c}\cap W^{c}}\sum_{\sigma\in I\cap(J\setminus\{j\})}\sum_{i\in(I_{1}\cup I_{2}\cup\{j-(n+1)\}\setminus\{\sigma\})^{c}}
(264) e˘(I1∪I2+n+1∪{j,i,i+n+1})∖{σ}∧e˘(J∪{n+1})∖{σ,j}+2​n+1.\displaystyle\qquad\breve{e}_{(I_{1}\cup I_{2}+n+1\cup\{j,i,i+n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma\}}\wedge\breve{e}_{(J\cup\{n+1\})\setminus\{\sigma,j\}+2n+1}.

References

Appendix A Existence of lattice

In Example 3.2, we assumed the existence of a lattice Γ\Gamma with the desired properties. It turns out that determining when such lattices exist in this context (and in broader contexts) is an active area of research. In dimension three, lattices related to affine structures are called affine crystallographic groups; a comprehensive discussion on this topic can be found in [Fried_1980]. For nilpotent and solvable manifolds, the existence of such lattices was studied in [Bock_2010] and [Andrada_2016].

Let GG be a simply connected Lie group. A complete left-invariant affine structure on GG is equivalent to an affine representation α:G→Aff⁡(ℝn)\alpha\colon G\to\operatorname{Aff}({\mathbb{R}}^{n}). Let ρ:G→GL​(n,ℝ)\rho\colon G\to\mathrm{GL}(n,{\mathbb{R}}) be the linear part of α\alpha, given by:

(265) ρ​(x1,x2,…,xn)=[100ex1​A].\rho(x_{1},x_{2},\ldots,x_{n})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\ 0&e^{x_{1}A}\end{bmatrix}.

In the almost abelian case, Bock [Bock_2010] proved the following theorem (see also [Andrada_2016, Proposition 2.3]).

Theorem 1.1 (Bock [Bock_2010], Andrada–Origlia [Andrada_2016]).  Let G=ℝ⋉ϕℝ2​n+1G={\mathbb{R}}\ltimes_{\phi}{\mathbb{R}}^{2n+1} be an almost abelian Lie group. Then GG admits a lattice if and only if there exists a t0≠0t_{0}\neq 0 such that ϕ​(t0)\phi(t_{0}) is conjugate to an integer matrix. The lattice is given by Γ=t0​ℤ⋉ϕP−1​ℤ2​n+1\Gamma=t_{0}{\mathbb{Z}}\ltimes_{\phi}P^{-1}{\mathbb{Z}}^{2n+1}, where P​ϕ​(t0)​P−1P\phi(t_{0})P^{-1} is an integer matrix.

Using this theorem, we are able to prove the following result, which asserts the existence of a lattice such that the left-invariant complete affine structure induced on the quotient G/ΓG/\Gamma is integral.

Theorem 1.2.  Let G=ℝ⋉ex1​Aℝ2​n+1G={\mathbb{R}}\ltimes_{e^{x_{1}A}}{\mathbb{R}}^{2n+1} be an almost abelian Lie group. Assume there exists a real number t0∈ℝ∖{0}t_{0}\in{\mathbb{R}}\setminus\{0\} and a matrix P∈GL​(2​n+1,ℝ)P\in\mathrm{GL}(2n+1,{\mathbb{R}}) such that et0​Ae^{t_{0}A} is conjugate to an integer matrix via PP.

Consider a complete left-invariant affine structure on GG given by the affine representation α:G→Aff⁡(ℝ2​n+2)\alpha\colon G\to\operatorname{Aff}({\mathbb{R}}^{2n+2}), whose linear part ρ:G→GL​(2​n+2,ℝ)\rho\colon G\to\mathrm{GL}(2n+2,{\mathbb{R}}) is given by:

(266) ρ​(x1,…,x2​n+2)=[100ex1​A].\rho(x_{1},\ldots,x_{2n+2})=\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\ 0&e^{x_{1}A}\end{bmatrix}.

Then, there is a lattice Γ=t0​ℤ⋉ex1​AP−1​ℤ2​n+1\Gamma=t_{0}{\mathbb{Z}}\ltimes_{e^{x_{1}A}}P^{-1}{\mathbb{Z}}^{2n+1} such that the affine structure induced on the solvmanifold G/ΓG/\Gamma is integral.

Proof.

Since et0​Ae^{t_{0}A} is conjugate to an integer matrix via PP, the preceding theorem guarantees the existence of the lattice Γ=t0​ℤ⋉ex1​AP−1​ℤ2​n+1\Gamma=t_{0}{\mathbb{Z}}\ltimes_{e^{x_{1}A}}P^{-1}{\mathbb{Z}}^{2n+1}.

It remains to prove that the left-invariant affine structure induced on G/ΓG/\Gamma is integral. Since π1​(G/Γ)≅Γ\pi_{1}(G/\Gamma)\cong\Gamma, the holonomy of the affine structure is given by the restriction α|Γ:Γ→Aff⁡(ℝ2​n+2){\left.\kern-1.2pt\alpha\mathchoice{\vphantom{\big|}}{}{}{}\right|_{\Gamma}}\colon\Gamma\to\operatorname{Aff}({\mathbb{R}}^{2n+2}), whose linear part is ρ|Γ{\left.\kern-1.2pt\rho\mathchoice{\vphantom{\big|}}{}{}{}\right|_{\Gamma}}. To prove that the induced affine structure is integral, we need to show that the image of ρ|Γ{\left.\kern-1.2pt\rho\mathchoice{\vphantom{\big|}}{}{}{}\right|_{\Gamma}} is conjugate to a subgroup of GL​(2​n+2,ℤ)\mathrm{GL}(2n+2,{\mathbb{Z}}).

Let (x1,x2,…,x2​n+2)∈Γ(x_{1},x_{2},\ldots,x_{2n+2})\in\Gamma. By the definition of the lattice, the first coordinate must be an integer multiple of t0t_{0}, meaning x1=k​t0x_{1}=kt_{0} for some k∈ℤk\in{\mathbb{Z}}. The linear part evaluated on this element is ρ​(k​t0,…)=diag​(1,ek​t0​A)\rho(kt_{0},\ldots)=\mathrm{diag}(1,e^{kt_{0}A}).

We conjugate this representation using the block diagonal matrix Ξ=[100P]\Xi=\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\ 0&P\end{bmatrix}:

(267) Ξ​ρ​(x1,…,x2​n+2)​Ξ−1=[100P]​[100ek​t0​A]​[100P−1]=[100P​ek​t0​A​P−1]=[100(P​et0​A​P−1)k].\Xi\,\rho(x_{1},\ldots,x_{2n+2})\,\Xi^{-1}=\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\ 0&P\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\ 0&e^{kt_{0}A}\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\ 0&P^{-1}\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\ 0&Pe^{kt_{0}A}P^{-1}\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\ 0&(Pe^{t_{0}A}P^{-1})^{k}\end{bmatrix}.

Since P​et0​A​P−1Pe^{t_{0}A}P^{-1} is an integer matrix by hypothesis, its kk-th power is also an integer matrix. Thus, the linear holonomy ρ|Γ{\left.\kern-1.2pt\rho\mathchoice{\vphantom{\big|}}{}{}{}\right|_{\Gamma}} is conjugate to a matrix in GL​(2​n+2,ℤ)\mathrm{GL}(2n+2,{\mathbb{Z}}). Therefore, the left-invariant affine structure induced by α\alpha on G/ΓG/\Gamma is complete and integral. ∎

Experimental support, please view the build logs for errors. Generated by L A T E xml .

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.

Have a free development cycle? Help support accessibility at arXiv! Our collaborators at LaTeXML maintain a list of packages that need conversion, and welcome developer contributions.

BETA
